[SOURCE: 2000_lavandier_Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution.pdf | PAGE: 1] [DIAGRAM / FIGURE / IMAGE detected on this page - 2 image(s) found] Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution B. Lavandier *, J. Jossinet, D. Cathignol INSERM, Unite 281, 151 Cours Albert Thomas, 69424 Lyon Cedex 03, France Received 11 February 1999; received in revised form 4 April 2000 Abstract Acoustic pressure alters local electrical conductivity in tissues and solutions. This work concerns the measurement of electrical conductivity in a liquid which is subjected to an acoustic pressure ®eld created by a focused transducer.
Jossinet, D. Cathignol INSERM, Unite 281, 151 Cours Albert Thomas, 69424 Lyon Cedex 03, France Received 11 February 1999; received in revised form 4 April 2000 Abstract Acoustic pressure alters local electrical conductivity in tissues and solutions. This work concerns the measurement of electrical conductivity in a liquid which is subjected to an acoustic pressure ®eld created by a focused transducer. Measurements were made with four electrodes positioned in the ultrasonic focal zone, and the signal concerned is referred to as the acousto-electric interaction signal. A solution of sodium chloride in a measurement cell was subjected to ultrasound pressures of upto 1 MPa.
This work concerns the measurement of electrical conductivity in a liquid which is subjected to an acoustic pressure ®eld created by a focused transducer. Measurements were made with four electrodes positioned in the ultrasonic focal zone, and the signal concerned is referred to as the acousto-electric interaction signal. A solution of sodium chloride in a measurement cell was subjected to ultrasound pressures of upto 1 MPa. It was shown that it is possible to quantitate the acousto-electric interaction signal once the ultrasonic vibration potential due to the Debye eect has been subtracted. The acousto-electric interaction signal was shown to be directly proportional to both the applied acoustic pressure and current. For the measurement cell used in this work, the interaction factor was found to be 5.3 lV mAÿ1 MPaÿ1.
It was shown that it is possible to quantitate the acousto-electric interaction signal once the ultrasonic vibration potential due to the Debye eect has been subtracted. The acousto-electric interaction signal was shown to be directly proportional to both the applied acoustic pressure and current. For the measurement cell used in this work, the interaction factor was found to be 5.3 lV mAÿ1 MPaÿ1. Ó 2000 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved. PACS: 43.35.Ty; 43.58.Wc Keywords: Ultrasound; Electrical conductivity; Electrolyte; Aqueous solution; Pressure; Ultrasonic vibrational potential 1. Introduction The eects of high-intensity therapeutic ultrasound on biological media, particularly cavitation and thermal eects, have been widely investigated.
All rights reserved. PACS: 43.35.Ty; 43.58.Wc Keywords: Ultrasound; Electrical conductivity; Electrolyte; Aqueous solution; Pressure; Ultrasonic vibrational potential 1. Introduction The eects of high-intensity therapeutic ultrasound on biological media, particularly cavitation and thermal eects, have been widely investigated. However, other eects can also be produced by relatively low-intensity ultrasound. A variety of biological eects have been discussed in the literature. Ultrasound is known to produce irre- versible cell damage [1,2]. Ultrasound can promote the healing of soft tissue [3] and bone repair [4] and can be used to stimulate nerve cells [5]. Ultrasound can induce a wide range of physical eects such as platelet aggrega- tion [6], or platelet orientation [7].
Ultrasound is known to produce irre- versible cell damage [1,2]. Ultrasound can promote the healing of soft tissue [3] and bone repair [4] and can be used to stimulate nerve cells [5]. Ultrasound can induce a wide range of physical eects such as platelet aggrega- tion [6], or platelet orientation [7]. Ultrasound also mediates subtle eects on conductive media through adiabatic compression eects: both pressure and tem- perature aect the electrical conductivity of electrolytes [8,9]. The propagation of an acoustic wave induces local changes in conductivity so that a current passing through an electrolyte can be modulated by an acoustic ®eld; this eect is referred to as the acousto-electrical interaction (AEI) signal. This ®eld has not been exten- sively investigated and is only touched upon in a handful of papers.
Ultrasound also mediates subtle eects on conductive media through adiabatic compression eects: both pressure and tem- perature aect the electrical conductivity of electrolytes [8,9]. The propagation of an acoustic wave induces local changes in conductivity so that a current passing through an electrolyte can be modulated by an acoustic ®eld; this eect is referred to as the acousto-electrical interaction (AEI) signal. This ®eld has not been exten- sively investigated and is only touched upon in a handful of papers. In a direct application of this eect, making pressure measurements using a cell containing a con- ducting electrolyte solution has been proposed [10]. Certain authors have speculated that this eect might be exploited to stimulate the brain in a highly localized fashion [11,12].
This ®eld has not been exten- sively investigated and is only touched upon in a handful of papers. In a direct application of this eect, making pressure measurements using a cell containing a con- ducting electrolyte solution has been proposed [10]. Certain authors have speculated that this eect might be exploited to stimulate the brain in a highly localized fashion [11,12]. There are several diculties associated with mea- suring the AEI signal. The ®rst diculty results from the fact that only a very small change in conductivity is induced by a change in pressure (e.g. 0.1% per MPa for NaCl solution); therefore, the AEI signal has a very low amplitude. The second problem is a consequence of one of the other eects of ultrasound, the ultrasonic vibrational potential (UVP) or Debye eect [13±15].
The ®rst diculty results from the fact that only a very small change in conductivity is induced by a change in pressure (e.g. 0.1% per MPa for NaCl solution); therefore, the AEI signal has a very low amplitude. The second problem is a consequence of one of the other eects of ultrasound, the ultrasonic vibrational potential (UVP) or Debye eect [13±15]. Without any current being applied, acoustic pressure changes induce a potential dierence, the amplitude of which is also a function of pressure. Thus, the AEI signal readings can be distorted by Debye potential dierences. The relationship of the AEI signal to the applied current and pressure was investigated. 2. Principle A uniform conducting medium with a conductivity r0 contains two electrodes of area S, separated by a dis- tance l (Fig. 1).
2. Principle A uniform conducting medium with a conductivity r0 contains two electrodes of area S, separated by a dis- tance l (Fig. 1). These electrodes are used to pass a constant current i through the medium. Assuming that the paths of the current are parallel to the axis formed by the electrodes, the voltage v at the electrode terminals is given by v  i 1 r0 l S  R0i; 1 where R0  1 r0 l S 2 and R0 is the resistance experienced by the electrodes. The application of an ultrasound ®eld induces a pressure variation dp which causes a change in con- ductivity given by r  r0  dr: 3 In consequence, there is a voltage change dv given by dv  ÿi dr r2 0 l S : 4 Since the change in conductivity is proportional to the change in pressure [16], Eq. 4 can be rewritten as dv  kiR0 dp: 5 As shown by Eq.
Assuming that the paths of the current are parallel to the axis formed by the electrodes, the voltage v at the electrode terminals is given by v  i 1 r0 l S  R0i; 1 where R0  1 r0 l S 2 and R0 is the resistance experienced by the electrodes. The application of an ultrasound ®eld induces a pressure variation dp which causes a change in con- ductivity given by r  r0  dr: 3 In consequence, there is a voltage change dv given by dv  ÿi dr r2 0 l S : 4 Since the change in conductivity is proportional to the change in pressure [16], Eq. 4 can be rewritten as dv  kiR0 dp: 5 As shown by Eq. 5, any ultrasound-induced change in conductivity gives rise to a change in voltage, i.e. the AEI signal, which is proportional to both the magnitude of the pressure change and the amount of current ¯owing through the medium.
4 can be rewritten as dv  kiR0 dp: 5 As shown by Eq. 5, any ultrasound-induced change in conductivity gives rise to a change in voltage, i.e. the AEI signal, which is proportional to both the magnitude of the pressure change and the amount of current ¯owing through the medium. If the pressure change dp is of the form dp  dp0 cosxt; 6 a voltage dv will be generated at the same frequency as the pressure signal. 3. Materials 3.1. Experimental set-up To detect changes in electrical conductivity induced by changes in acoustic pressure, the experimental set-up shown in Fig. 2 was used. A measurement cell contain- ing electrodes and a conducting solution was immersed in a large, rectangular tank with internal dimensions of 23 cm (width)  30 cm (height)  52 cm (length) ®lled with degassed water.
Experimental set-up To detect changes in electrical conductivity induced by changes in acoustic pressure, the experimental set-up shown in Fig. 2 was used. A measurement cell contain- ing electrodes and a conducting solution was immersed in a large, rectangular tank with internal dimensions of 23 cm (width)  30 cm (height)  52 cm (length) ®lled with degassed water. A focused transducer was mounted on one of the smallest sides of this tank. An absorbing surface was mounted opposite the transducer to prevent standing waves. All measurements were made at room temperature 20  2°C. 3.2. Ultrasound generator All measurements were made using a 500 kHz transducer, 50 mm in diameter and focused at 165 mm. A quarter wave plate was used for acoustic matching and the transducer impedance was electrically matched to 50 X by a transformer.
3.2. Ultrasound generator All measurements were made using a 500 kHz transducer, 50 mm in diameter and focused at 165 mm. A quarter wave plate was used for acoustic matching and the transducer impedance was electrically matched to 50 X by a transformer. The transducer drive power was pulsed in order to generate high acoustic pressure levels without overheating the solution and running the risk of cavitation. This supply wasgenerated by a func- tion generator (Hewlett Packard type 8116) operating in burst mode. The power driving the emission of ultra- sound waves comprised 10 sinusoidal periods at a fre- quency of 500 kHz. The recurrence frequency for the bursts was 100 Hz. This function generator was con- nected to an ampli®er (KALMUS type 150C). This set- Fig. 1. Principle of acousto-electric interaction. Fig. 2.
Principle of acousto-electric interaction. Fig. 2. The experimental set-up. 930 B. Lavandier et al. / Ultrasonics 38 (2000) 929±936  [SOURCE: 2000_lavandier_Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution.pdf | PAGE: 3] [DIAGRAM / FIGURE / IMAGE detected on this page - 2 image(s) found] [FIGURE / TABLE caption detected on this page] up gave pressures of the order of 1 MPa at the focal point of the transducer, as shown in the pressure dia- gram (Fig. 3) which was obtained using a PVDF mem- brane hydrophone with an active element of 0.5 mm (Marconi). 3.3. The measurement cell The measurement cell (Fig. 4) was made up of a Perspex tube (internal diameter 90 mm, thickness 5 mm and length 20 mm) containing the electrodes.
3.3. The measurement cell The measurement cell (Fig. 4) was made up of a Perspex tube (internal diameter 90 mm, thickness 5 mm and length 20 mm) containing the electrodes. Its two sides were formed by stretching and gluing on latex membranes to act as an acoustic window. The sodium chloride solution (9 g/l) was introduced into the cell through two silicon tubes, and once it had been ®lled, it was suspended in the tank full of degassed water. Mi- cromanipulators with a track of 18 mm allowed dis- placement of the cell through the ultrasound ®eld along X, Y and Z axes, and the tank had a positioning system to allow adjustment of its orientation in the ultrasound ®eld. Two dierent pairs of electrodes were used; one pair for supplying the current and the other pair for measuring the voltage.
The sodium chloride solution (9 g/l) was introduced into the cell through two silicon tubes, and once it had been ®lled, it was suspended in the tank full of degassed water. Mi- cromanipulators with a track of 18 mm allowed dis- placement of the cell through the ultrasound ®eld along X, Y and Z axes, and the tank had a positioning system to allow adjustment of its orientation in the ultrasound ®eld. Two dierent pairs of electrodes were used; one pair for supplying the current and the other pair for measuring the voltage. This is a usual practice in the domain of making measurements of biological imped- ance, mainly because it avoids all the phenomena as- sociated with polarization of the electrodes.
Mi- cromanipulators with a track of 18 mm allowed dis- placement of the cell through the ultrasound ®eld along X, Y and Z axes, and the tank had a positioning system to allow adjustment of its orientation in the ultrasound ®eld. Two dierent pairs of electrodes were used; one pair for supplying the current and the other pair for measuring the voltage. This is a usual practice in the domain of making measurements of biological imped- ance, mainly because it avoids all the phenomena as- sociated with polarization of the electrodes. In fact, for this work in particular, any eect that the ultrasound might have on electrode polarization impedance would not have had any great impact on the measurements. The electrodes consisted of four parallel and aligned stainless steel rods, 1 mm in diameter and 78 mm in total length.
This is a usual practice in the domain of making measurements of biological imped- ance, mainly because it avoids all the phenomena as- sociated with polarization of the electrodes. In fact, for this work in particular, any eect that the ultrasound might have on electrode polarization impedance would not have had any great impact on the measurements. The electrodes consisted of four parallel and aligned stainless steel rods, 1 mm in diameter and 78 mm in total length. They were located in the middle of the measurement chamber, perpendicular to the beam axis and in symmetrical positions with respect to the latter. The outermost electrodes were used for current injec- tion and the other two for voltage sensing.
The electrodes consisted of four parallel and aligned stainless steel rods, 1 mm in diameter and 78 mm in total length. They were located in the middle of the measurement chamber, perpendicular to the beam axis and in symmetrical positions with respect to the latter. The outermost electrodes were used for current injec- tion and the other two for voltage sensing. Only their central part was active and they were covered with an insulating polyethylene sheath with an external diameter of 1.6 mm. The current supply electrodes had an active length of 10 mm and were separated by a distance of 7 mm. The voltage measurement electrodes had an ac- tive length of 3 mm and were separated by a distance of 3 mm.
Only their central part was active and they were covered with an insulating polyethylene sheath with an external diameter of 1.6 mm. The current supply electrodes had an active length of 10 mm and were separated by a distance of 7 mm. The voltage measurement electrodes had an ac- tive length of 3 mm and were separated by a distance of 3 mm. To keep the measuring wires out of the ultra- sound ®eld, they were soldered onto the ends of the electrodes which were stuck inside the Perspex cylin- ders. These cylinders were ®xed into special holes bored into the tube which made up the framework of the tank. 3.4. Current generator In purely theoretical terms, to detect the AEI signal, it would be ideal to be able to use direct current to obtain a high-frequency signal.
These cylinders were ®xed into special holes bored into the tube which made up the framework of the tank. 3.4. Current generator In purely theoretical terms, to detect the AEI signal, it would be ideal to be able to use direct current to obtain a high-frequency signal. However, direct current has certain major disadvantages in that it induces elec- trode polarization which is exacerbated by the eects of electrolysis. In order to avoid these problems, it was decided to work with low-frequency alternating current with a symmetrical, rectangular wave form with a fre- quency of 100 Hz.
Current generator In purely theoretical terms, to detect the AEI signal, it would be ideal to be able to use direct current to obtain a high-frequency signal. However, direct current has certain major disadvantages in that it induces elec- trode polarization which is exacerbated by the eects of electrolysis. In order to avoid these problems, it was decided to work with low-frequency alternating current with a symmetrical, rectangular wave form with a fre- quency of 100 Hz. The signal generator (Hewlett Packard type 8112) used for this current was synchronized with the ultra- sound generator in such a way to ensure that the ul- trasound pulse was emitted 2.5 ms after the ascending front of the low-frequency signal so that any transient electrical phenomena would have disappeared by the time the pressure wave arrived at the measurement area.
However, direct current has certain major disadvantages in that it induces elec- trode polarization which is exacerbated by the eects of electrolysis. In order to avoid these problems, it was decided to work with low-frequency alternating current with a symmetrical, rectangular wave form with a fre- quency of 100 Hz. The signal generator (Hewlett Packard type 8112) used for this current was synchronized with the ultra- sound generator in such a way to ensure that the ul- trasound pulse was emitted 2.5 ms after the ascending front of the low-frequency signal so that any transient electrical phenomena would have disappeared by the time the pressure wave arrived at the measurement area. A voltage±current converter driven by the rectangular signal was used to maintain a constant current (12 mA maximum value) through the solution.
In order to avoid these problems, it was decided to work with low-frequency alternating current with a symmetrical, rectangular wave form with a fre- quency of 100 Hz. The signal generator (Hewlett Packard type 8112) used for this current was synchronized with the ultra- sound generator in such a way to ensure that the ul- trasound pulse was emitted 2.5 ms after the ascending front of the low-frequency signal so that any transient electrical phenomena would have disappeared by the time the pressure wave arrived at the measurement area. A voltage±current converter driven by the rectangular signal was used to maintain a constant current (12 mA maximum value) through the solution. The direction of Fig. 3. Lavandier et al. / Ultrasonics 38 (2000) 929±936 931  [SOURCE: 2000_lavandier_Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution.pdf | PAGE: 4] [DIAGRAM / FIGURE / IMAGE detected on this page - 1 image(s) found] [FIGURE / TABLE caption detected on this page] the current could be reversed by reversing the polarity of the signal generator. 3.5. Signal measurement The voltage measurement electrodes were connected to the input of a specially fabricated dierential ampli- ®er designed for electrical impedance tomography [17,18]. This ampli®er had a gain of 100, a )6db band- width of 2 MHz, a dierential input impedance of 660 kX and a shared mode rejection rate of 60 dB.
3.5. Signal measurement The voltage measurement electrodes were connected to the input of a specially fabricated dierential ampli- ®er designed for electrical impedance tomography [17,18]. This ampli®er had a gain of 100, a )6db band- width of 2 MHz, a dierential input impedance of 660 kX and a shared mode rejection rate of 60 dB. After the ampli®er, there was a pass-band ®lter with a central frequency of 500 kHz and a )6db bandwidth of 100 kHz. The signals coming from the ampli®er and the ®lter were passed into a digital oscillator (Tektronix 2430A) with a sampling frequency of 100 MHz. In order to re- duce the background noise, the signals measured were averaged using the oscilloscopeÕs internal averaging system.
After the ampli®er, there was a pass-band ®lter with a central frequency of 500 kHz and a )6db bandwidth of 100 kHz. The signals coming from the ampli®er and the ®lter were passed into a digital oscillator (Tektronix 2430A) with a sampling frequency of 100 MHz. In order to re- duce the background noise, the signals measured were averaged using the oscilloscopeÕs internal averaging system. All signals measured on the oscilloscope could be printed out from the plotter or recorded on diskette via the IEEE interface. 4. Methods The goal was to investigate the feasibility of mea- suring the AEI signal and then follow its variation with pressure and current. The measurement cell was ®lled with a 0.9% sodium chloride solution and submerged in the tank.
4. Methods The goal was to investigate the feasibility of mea- suring the AEI signal and then follow its variation with pressure and current. The measurement cell was ®lled with a 0.9% sodium chloride solution and submerged in the tank. It was oriented so that the plane of the electrodes was per- pendicular to the ultrasound propagation axis, and the micromanipulators were used to position it with its center (i.e. the active part of the measurement elec- trodes) within the transducerÕs focal area. A symmetri- cal, rectangular 12 mA current was passed between the electrodes, and the measurement cell experienced a si- nusoidal acoustic pressure of an amplitude of 0.92 MPa as measured at the focal point. A signal was observed on the oscilloscope screen but the background noise was too high.
the active part of the measurement elec- trodes) within the transducerÕs focal area. A symmetri- cal, rectangular 12 mA current was passed between the electrodes, and the measurement cell experienced a si- nusoidal acoustic pressure of an amplitude of 0.92 MPa as measured at the focal point. A signal was observed on the oscilloscope screen but the background noise was too high. When the oscilloscopeÕs internal averaging system was activated, the signal became much clearer and it was observed at the moment at which the ultra- sound pressure wave reached the measurement zone de®ned by the electrodes. As can be seen in Fig. 5, the form and duration of the signal closely correspond to the acoustic pressure pattern.
When the oscilloscopeÕs internal averaging system was activated, the signal became much clearer and it was observed at the moment at which the ultra- sound pressure wave reached the measurement zone de®ned by the electrodes. As can be seen in Fig. 5, the form and duration of the signal closely correspond to the acoustic pressure pattern. The point at which the signal was largest was deter- mined by adjusting the orientation and varying the measurement cellÕs position (using the X and Y micro- manipulators). This point was taken as the reference position for all the measurements discussed in this work. It was observed that the maximum was not obtained when the direction of the current was perpendicular to the ultrasound propagation axis, but rather at an angle of about 80° to it.
The point at which the signal was largest was deter- mined by adjusting the orientation and varying the measurement cellÕs position (using the X and Y micro- manipulators). This point was taken as the reference position for all the measurements discussed in this work. It was observed that the maximum was not obtained when the direction of the current was perpendicular to the ultrasound propagation axis, but rather at an angle of about 80° to it. In order to check that the observed signal was indeed due to the ultrasound, several basic tests were con- ducted: · Ultrasound emission was interrupted; this caused the signal to disappear. · Using the micromanipulators, the measurement cell was displaced a known distance along the ultrasound propagation axis; the signal was observed to be de- layed by the appropriate time factor.
It was observed that the maximum was not obtained when the direction of the current was perpendicular to the ultrasound propagation axis, but rather at an angle of about 80° to it. In order to check that the observed signal was indeed due to the ultrasound, several basic tests were con- ducted: · Ultrasound emission was interrupted; this caused the signal to disappear. · Using the micromanipulators, the measurement cell was displaced a known distance along the ultrasound propagation axis; the signal was observed to be de- layed by the appropriate time factor. · Using the micromanipulators, the measurement cell was displaced a known distance along the X-axis in order to move it out of the focal plane; the amplitude of the signal was observed to diminish.
In order to check that the observed signal was indeed due to the ultrasound, several basic tests were con- ducted: · Ultrasound emission was interrupted; this caused the signal to disappear. · Using the micromanipulators, the measurement cell was displaced a known distance along the ultrasound propagation axis; the signal was observed to be de- layed by the appropriate time factor. · Using the micromanipulators, the measurement cell was displaced a known distance along the X-axis in order to move it out of the focal plane; the amplitude of the signal was observed to diminish. · The phase of the ultrasound drive signal was reversed (a 180° phase change); the phase of the signal was ob- served to be reversed. In order to check that the observed signal was indeed dependent on the current, the direction of the current was reversed.
· Using the micromanipulators, the measurement cell was displaced a known distance along the X-axis in order to move it out of the focal plane; the amplitude of the signal was observed to diminish. · The phase of the ultrasound drive signal was reversed (a 180° phase change); the phase of the signal was ob- served to be reversed. In order to check that the observed signal was indeed dependent on the current, the direction of the current was reversed. Instead of the signal getting reversed, as expected, a major decrease in its amplitude was ob- served. The most likely explanation for this apparent asymmetry was that there was some other signal which was positively interfering with the signal due to acoustic pressure-dependent changes in conductivity.
In order to check that the observed signal was indeed dependent on the current, the direction of the current was reversed. Instead of the signal getting reversed, as expected, a major decrease in its amplitude was ob- served. The most likely explanation for this apparent asymmetry was that there was some other signal which was positively interfering with the signal due to acoustic pressure-dependent changes in conductivity. This hy- pothesis was con®rmed when the current between the electrodes was switched o and a signal corresponding to the acoustic pressure pattern was still detected. This signal is due to a Debye eect vibration potential at the electrodes in the ultrasound ®eld. Therefore, these two dierent signals, both of which were contributing to the readings, had to be resolved.
This hy- pothesis was con®rmed when the current between the electrodes was switched o and a signal corresponding to the acoustic pressure pattern was still detected. This signal is due to a Debye eect vibration potential at the electrodes in the ultrasound ®eld. Therefore, these two dierent signals, both of which were contributing to the readings, had to be resolved. Assuming that the Debye eect was not aected by the current passing between the electrodes, it was decided to Fig. 5. Lavandier et al. / Ultrasonics 38 (2000) 929±936  [SOURCE: 2000_lavandier_Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution.pdf | PAGE: 5] [DIAGRAM / FIGURE / IMAGE detected on this page - 2 image(s) found] [FIGURE / TABLE caption detected on this page] make two separate, successive measurements of the signals at the electrode terminals for every data point: ®rst, to quantitate the Debye eect signal, in the pres- ence of an ultrasound ®eld but without any current passing through the solution, and second, with both the ultrasound ®eld and the current. The signal resulting from acousto-electric interactions (i.e.
Lavandier et al. / Ultrasonics 38 (2000) 929±936  [SOURCE: 2000_lavandier_Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution.pdf | PAGE: 5] [DIAGRAM / FIGURE / IMAGE detected on this page - 2 image(s) found] [FIGURE / TABLE caption detected on this page] make two separate, successive measurements of the signals at the electrode terminals for every data point: ®rst, to quantitate the Debye eect signal, in the pres- ence of an ultrasound ®eld but without any current passing through the solution, and second, with both the ultrasound ®eld and the current. The signal resulting from acousto-electric interactions (i.e. between the ul- trasound waves and the current) corresponds to the dierence between these two measurements.
/ Ultrasonics 38 (2000) 929±936  [SOURCE: 2000_lavandier_Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution.pdf | PAGE: 5] [DIAGRAM / FIGURE / IMAGE detected on this page - 2 image(s) found] [FIGURE / TABLE caption detected on this page] make two separate, successive measurements of the signals at the electrode terminals for every data point: ®rst, to quantitate the Debye eect signal, in the pres- ence of an ultrasound ®eld but without any current passing through the solution, and second, with both the ultrasound ®eld and the current. The signal resulting from acousto-electric interactions (i.e. between the ul- trasound waves and the current) corresponds to the dierence between these two measurements. Results are presented after ampli®cation and ®ltering, and all results pertaining to the voltage at the electrode terminals have been normalized by dividing the mea- sured signal by the gain of the instrument used.
The signal resulting from acousto-electric interactions (i.e. between the ul- trasound waves and the current) corresponds to the dierence between these two measurements. Results are presented after ampli®cation and ®ltering, and all results pertaining to the voltage at the electrode terminals have been normalized by dividing the mea- sured signal by the gain of the instrument used. 5. Results 5.1. Measurements (taking the Debye eect voltage into account) The signal at the electrode terminals was measured, without any current and with the measurement cell at dierent positions along the X-axis, for a series of dif- ferent acoustic pressure levels. Fig. 6 shows the variation of the signal as a function of the position on the X-axis at a series of dierent focal point acoustic pressure lev- els.
Measurements (taking the Debye eect voltage into account) The signal at the electrode terminals was measured, without any current and with the measurement cell at dierent positions along the X-axis, for a series of dif- ferent acoustic pressure levels. Fig. 6 shows the variation of the signal as a function of the position on the X-axis at a series of dierent focal point acoustic pressure lev- els. These readings gave curves which corresponded to the o-axis pressure ®eld distribution. For a focal pressure of 0.92 MPa, electrode terminal voltages were measured with a current of 12 mA passing in both the directions with the measurement cell at dif- ferent positions along the X-axis. Fig. 7(a) shows the amplitude of the signal as a function of the cell position with and without applied current.
For a focal pressure of 0.92 MPa, electrode terminal voltages were measured with a current of 12 mA passing in both the directions with the measurement cell at dif- ferent positions along the X-axis. Fig. 7(a) shows the amplitude of the signal as a function of the cell position with and without applied current. In order to determine the signal resulting from acousto-electrical interac- tions, the signal measured in the absence of any current (i.e. the signal due to the Debye eect) was subtracted from that measured with the current ¯owing. Fig. 7(b) shows the resultant curve as a function of the mea- surement cellÕs position along the X-axis: data points generated with the current moving in each direction form quasi-symmetrical curves both of which resemble o-axis pressure ®eld distribution of the transducer.
the signal due to the Debye eect) was subtracted from that measured with the current ¯owing. Fig. 7(b) shows the resultant curve as a function of the mea- surement cellÕs position along the X-axis: data points generated with the current moving in each direction form quasi-symmetrical curves both of which resemble o-axis pressure ®eld distribution of the transducer. Henceforth, only resultant signals (i.e. ``the signal measured in the presence of current'' minus ``the signal measured without any current'') will be presented. 5.2.
Henceforth, only resultant signals (i.e. ``the signal measured in the presence of current'' minus ``the signal measured without any current'') will be presented. 5.2. Relationship between AEI signal and pressure In order to follow the variation of interaction signal with acoustic pressure, the signal at the voltage electrode terminals was measured ± with the measurement cell at dierent positions along the X-axis ± at a series of dierent energy levels corresponding to focal point pressure readings of 0.37, 0.63, 0.92 and 1 MPa. Measurements were made for currents of 1.5, 3, 6 and 12 mA traveling in each direction. In Fig. 8, the Fig. 6. UVP diagrams for dierent focal pressures. Fig. 7.

At each dierent current value, the readings fall on a line, and linear regression analysis, in every case, yielded a straight line, which passed close to the origin and gave a correlation coecient of over 0.95. As far as the slope of these linear regression analyses is concerned, for every doubling of the current, the linear regression changed by more or less the same factor. 5.3.
At each dierent current value, the readings fall on a line, and linear regression analysis, in every case, yielded a straight line, which passed close to the origin and gave a correlation coecient of over 0.95. As far as the slope of these linear regression analyses is concerned, for every doubling of the current, the linear regression changed by more or less the same factor. 5.3. Relationship between AEI signal and current Taking only those results pertaining to measurements made at the focal point, the variation of AEI signal with current is shown in Fig. 9. The direction of the current for which the measured electrode terminal voltage was greater than the Debye eect voltage was considered as positive.
Relationship between AEI signal and current Taking only those results pertaining to measurements made at the focal point, the variation of AEI signal with current is shown in Fig. 9. The direction of the current for which the measured electrode terminal voltage was greater than the Debye eect voltage was considered as positive. At each of the four dierent pressure levels, all of the experimentally measured points fall on a line and linear regression analysis, in every case, yielded a straight line which passed close to the origin and gave a correlation coecient of over 0.99. The slopes of the various linear regression analyses were all more or less proportional to pressure. 5.4.
At each of the four dierent pressure levels, all of the experimentally measured points fall on a line and linear regression analysis, in every case, yielded a straight line which passed close to the origin and gave a correlation coecient of over 0.99. The slopes of the various linear regression analyses were all more or less proportional to pressure. 5.4. Determination of an apparent interaction factor The fact that the interaction voltage is proportional to both pressure and current makes it possible to derive an apparent interaction factor. For every single data point, the ratio of the interaction voltage to the acoustic pressure was calculated and plotted against current (Fig. 10). Linear regression analysis of these data gives a straight line which passes close to the origin and gives a correlation coecient of over 0.97.
For every single data point, the ratio of the interaction voltage to the acoustic pressure was calculated and plotted against current (Fig. 10). Linear regression analysis of these data gives a straight line which passes close to the origin and gives a correlation coecient of over 0.97. The slope of this line is the apparent interaction factor (5.3 lV mAÿ1 MPaÿ1). Fig. 9. Amplitude of the AEI signal as a function of current for dif- ferent focal pressure values. The current is arbitrarily de®ned as pos- itive when the measured signal voltage is greater than the UVP signal value. Fig. 10. Ratio of the interaction voltage to the acoustic pressure as a function of current. Fig. 8. Amplitude of the AEI signal as a function of acoustic pressure for dierent current values. 934 B. Lavandier et al. / Ultrasonics 38 (2000) 929±936  [SOURCE: 2000_lavandier_Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution.pdf | PAGE: 7] 6. Discussion and conclusions In the present experimental conditions, the voltage between the electrodes was the addition of two eects: the changes in the conductivity of the medium due to the presence of a pressure wave and the vibration potential due to the Debye eect. Both these voltages depend on acoustic pressure, and in order to derive only the AEI signal, it is necessary to measure and subtract the Debye component (which is present whether or not there is any current) from the overall voltage measured experimen- tally when a current is ¯owing.
/ Ultrasonics 38 (2000) 929±936  [SOURCE: 2000_lavandier_Experimental measurement of the acousto-electric interaction signal in saline solution.pdf | PAGE: 7] 6. Discussion and conclusions In the present experimental conditions, the voltage between the electrodes was the addition of two eects: the changes in the conductivity of the medium due to the presence of a pressure wave and the vibration potential due to the Debye eect. Both these voltages depend on acoustic pressure, and in order to derive only the AEI signal, it is necessary to measure and subtract the Debye component (which is present whether or not there is any current) from the overall voltage measured experimen- tally when a current is ¯owing. We are not aware that attention has been drawn to this point previously.
Discussion and conclusions In the present experimental conditions, the voltage between the electrodes was the addition of two eects: the changes in the conductivity of the medium due to the presence of a pressure wave and the vibration potential due to the Debye eect. Both these voltages depend on acoustic pressure, and in order to derive only the AEI signal, it is necessary to measure and subtract the Debye component (which is present whether or not there is any current) from the overall voltage measured experimen- tally when a current is ¯owing. We are not aware that attention has been drawn to this point previously. Insofar as the direction of the current path with re- spect to the ultrasound propagation axis is concerned, it is certain that ®nding the best orientation for the mea- surement cell by identifying the point at which the voltage is highest is far from ideal, because the point thus identi®ed is only that where the sum of the Debye and acousto-electric interaction components is at its greatest.
Both these voltages depend on acoustic pressure, and in order to derive only the AEI signal, it is necessary to measure and subtract the Debye component (which is present whether or not there is any current) from the overall voltage measured experimen- tally when a current is ¯owing. We are not aware that attention has been drawn to this point previously. Insofar as the direction of the current path with re- spect to the ultrasound propagation axis is concerned, it is certain that ®nding the best orientation for the mea- surement cell by identifying the point at which the voltage is highest is far from ideal, because the point thus identi®ed is only that where the sum of the Debye and acousto-electric interaction components is at its greatest. Given the con®guration of the electrodes used, the ultrasonic vibration potential would be at a maxi- mum when the distance between the measurement elec- trodes projected onto the ultrasound propagation axis was equivalent to one half wavelength.
We are not aware that attention has been drawn to this point previously. Insofar as the direction of the current path with re- spect to the ultrasound propagation axis is concerned, it is certain that ®nding the best orientation for the mea- surement cell by identifying the point at which the voltage is highest is far from ideal, because the point thus identi®ed is only that where the sum of the Debye and acousto-electric interaction components is at its greatest. Given the con®guration of the electrodes used, the ultrasonic vibration potential would be at a maxi- mum when the distance between the measurement elec- trodes projected onto the ultrasound propagation axis was equivalent to one half wavelength. In this series of experiments, the plane of the electrodes was at a 60° angle to the ultrasound propagation axis, whereas the Debye eect is at a minimum if this angle is 90° (con- ®rmed experimentally).
Insofar as the direction of the current path with re- spect to the ultrasound propagation axis is concerned, it is certain that ®nding the best orientation for the mea- surement cell by identifying the point at which the voltage is highest is far from ideal, because the point thus identi®ed is only that where the sum of the Debye and acousto-electric interaction components is at its greatest. Given the con®guration of the electrodes used, the ultrasonic vibration potential would be at a maxi- mum when the distance between the measurement elec- trodes projected onto the ultrasound propagation axis was equivalent to one half wavelength. In this series of experiments, the plane of the electrodes was at a 60° angle to the ultrasound propagation axis, whereas the Debye eect is at a minimum if this angle is 90° (con- ®rmed experimentally). For the measurements made, the path of the current was not perpendicular to the ultra- sound propagation axis but rather at an angle of about 80°.
Given the con®guration of the electrodes used, the ultrasonic vibration potential would be at a maxi- mum when the distance between the measurement elec- trodes projected onto the ultrasound propagation axis was equivalent to one half wavelength. In this series of experiments, the plane of the electrodes was at a 60° angle to the ultrasound propagation axis, whereas the Debye eect is at a minimum if this angle is 90° (con- ®rmed experimentally). For the measurements made, the path of the current was not perpendicular to the ultra- sound propagation axis but rather at an angle of about 80°. Of course, if measurements had been made at 90°, the voltage due to changes in the conductivity of the solution would have been at a maximum [11], but still there would have been a Debye eect component, which would have been very small and therefore dicult to measure.
In this series of experiments, the plane of the electrodes was at a 60° angle to the ultrasound propagation axis, whereas the Debye eect is at a minimum if this angle is 90° (con- ®rmed experimentally). For the measurements made, the path of the current was not perpendicular to the ultra- sound propagation axis but rather at an angle of about 80°. Of course, if measurements had been made at 90°, the voltage due to changes in the conductivity of the solution would have been at a maximum [11], but still there would have been a Debye eect component, which would have been very small and therefore dicult to measure. In this case, the fact that such measurements can actually contain two dierent components would never have been detected.
For the measurements made, the path of the current was not perpendicular to the ultra- sound propagation axis but rather at an angle of about 80°. Of course, if measurements had been made at 90°, the voltage due to changes in the conductivity of the solution would have been at a maximum [11], but still there would have been a Debye eect component, which would have been very small and therefore dicult to measure. In this case, the fact that such measurements can actually contain two dierent components would never have been detected. With respect to the measurement volume de®ned by the two electrodes, the fact that it is not exactly de®ned in space, especially in the direction of ultrasound propa- gation, shows that the electrode con®guration chosen did not maximize the AEI signal due to pressure-induced changes in conductivity.
Of course, if measurements had been made at 90°, the voltage due to changes in the conductivity of the solution would have been at a maximum [11], but still there would have been a Debye eect component, which would have been very small and therefore dicult to measure. In this case, the fact that such measurements can actually contain two dierent components would never have been detected. With respect to the measurement volume de®ned by the two electrodes, the fact that it is not exactly de®ned in space, especially in the direction of ultrasound propa- gation, shows that the electrode con®guration chosen did not maximize the AEI signal due to pressure-induced changes in conductivity. Since successive positive and negative pressure waves induce both an increase and decrease in conductivity, it is possible that these might be sensed by the electrodes at the same time which would attenuate the AEI signal, possibly to a signi®cant extent.
In this case, the fact that such measurements can actually contain two dierent components would never have been detected. With respect to the measurement volume de®ned by the two electrodes, the fact that it is not exactly de®ned in space, especially in the direction of ultrasound propa- gation, shows that the electrode con®guration chosen did not maximize the AEI signal due to pressure-induced changes in conductivity. Since successive positive and negative pressure waves induce both an increase and decrease in conductivity, it is possible that these might be sensed by the electrodes at the same time which would attenuate the AEI signal, possibly to a signi®cant extent. For this reason, it is only possible to de®ne an apparent interaction factor which is speci®c to the con®guration of the electrodes used for these measurements.
With respect to the measurement volume de®ned by the two electrodes, the fact that it is not exactly de®ned in space, especially in the direction of ultrasound propa- gation, shows that the electrode con®guration chosen did not maximize the AEI signal due to pressure-induced changes in conductivity. Since successive positive and negative pressure waves induce both an increase and decrease in conductivity, it is possible that these might be sensed by the electrodes at the same time which would attenuate the AEI signal, possibly to a signi®cant extent. For this reason, it is only possible to de®ne an apparent interaction factor which is speci®c to the con®guration of the electrodes used for these measurements. It might be asked whether the presence of electrodes in the pressure ®eld might not give rise to electrode eects at the interfaces with the conducting solution.
Since successive positive and negative pressure waves induce both an increase and decrease in conductivity, it is possible that these might be sensed by the electrodes at the same time which would attenuate the AEI signal, possibly to a signi®cant extent. For this reason, it is only possible to de®ne an apparent interaction factor which is speci®c to the con®guration of the electrodes used for these measurements. It might be asked whether the presence of electrodes in the pressure ®eld might not give rise to electrode eects at the interfaces with the conducting solution. Given that a constant current is supplied to the external electrodes, any change in their polarization impedance would only induce minor voltage changes at the electrodes.
For this reason, it is only possible to de®ne an apparent interaction factor which is speci®c to the con®guration of the electrodes used for these measurements. It might be asked whether the presence of electrodes in the pressure ®eld might not give rise to electrode eects at the interfaces with the conducting solution. Given that a constant current is supplied to the external electrodes, any change in their polarization impedance would only induce minor voltage changes at the electrodes. Any such minor changes would have little impact on the mea- surement electrodes due to the use of the four-electrodes technique. Moreover, using a dierential measurement system tends to cancel out such electrode eects.
Given that a constant current is supplied to the external electrodes, any change in their polarization impedance would only induce minor voltage changes at the electrodes. Any such minor changes would have little impact on the mea- surement electrodes due to the use of the four-electrodes technique. Moreover, using a dierential measurement system tends to cancel out such electrode eects. With respect to ``false'' and ``junction eects'' like those involved in the measurement of the Debye eect and discussed by Rosenfeld et al. [19], Millner [14] and Zana [15], the fact that the electrodes used were rela- tively long means that the acoustic pressure at the points where they are ®xed into the framework is low (actually close to zero) which helps cut down ``false eects''.
Moreover, using a dierential measurement system tends to cancel out such electrode eects. With respect to ``false'' and ``junction eects'' like those involved in the measurement of the Debye eect and discussed by Rosenfeld et al. [19], Millner [14] and Zana [15], the fact that the electrodes used were rela- tively long means that the acoustic pressure at the points where they are ®xed into the framework is low (actually close to zero) which helps cut down ``false eects''. Moreover, the fact that the signal measured in the ab- sence of current is subtracted from the signal measured in the presence of current tends to reduce the impact of such ``false'' and ``junction eects'' on the ®nal result.
With respect to ``false'' and ``junction eects'' like those involved in the measurement of the Debye eect and discussed by Rosenfeld et al. [19], Millner [14] and Zana [15], the fact that the electrodes used were rela- tively long means that the acoustic pressure at the points where they are ®xed into the framework is low (actually close to zero) which helps cut down ``false eects''. Moreover, the fact that the signal measured in the ab- sence of current is subtracted from the signal measured in the presence of current tends to reduce the impact of such ``false'' and ``junction eects'' on the ®nal result. Heating of the medium would lead to variations in conductivity but these measurements were performed in a medium which absorbs ultrasound ineciently and with a wave pulse of short duration (20 ls) in compar- ison to its recurrence frequency (100 Hz) such that the solution temperature did not change signi®cantly.
[19], Millner [14] and Zana [15], the fact that the electrodes used were rela- tively long means that the acoustic pressure at the points where they are ®xed into the framework is low (actually close to zero) which helps cut down ``false eects''. Moreover, the fact that the signal measured in the ab- sence of current is subtracted from the signal measured in the presence of current tends to reduce the impact of such ``false'' and ``junction eects'' on the ®nal result. Heating of the medium would lead to variations in conductivity but these measurements were performed in a medium which absorbs ultrasound ineciently and with a wave pulse of short duration (20 ls) in compar- ison to its recurrence frequency (100 Hz) such that the solution temperature did not change signi®cantly. In conclusion, these experiments have shown that it is possible to measure the AEI signal which results from ultrasound-induced changes in the conductivity of a conducting solution.
Moreover, the fact that the signal measured in the ab- sence of current is subtracted from the signal measured in the presence of current tends to reduce the impact of such ``false'' and ``junction eects'' on the ®nal result. Heating of the medium would lead to variations in conductivity but these measurements were performed in a medium which absorbs ultrasound ineciently and with a wave pulse of short duration (20 ls) in compar- ison to its recurrence frequency (100 Hz) such that the solution temperature did not change signi®cantly. In conclusion, these experiments have shown that it is possible to measure the AEI signal which results from ultrasound-induced changes in the conductivity of a conducting solution. It has been shown that it is neces- sary to determine and subtract ultrasound vibration potential eects if the AEI signal due to conductivity changes is to be measured accurately.
Heating of the medium would lead to variations in conductivity but these measurements were performed in a medium which absorbs ultrasound ineciently and with a wave pulse of short duration (20 ls) in compar- ison to its recurrence frequency (100 Hz) such that the solution temperature did not change signi®cantly. In conclusion, these experiments have shown that it is possible to measure the AEI signal which results from ultrasound-induced changes in the conductivity of a conducting solution. It has been shown that it is neces- sary to determine and subtract ultrasound vibration potential eects if the AEI signal due to conductivity changes is to be measured accurately. These results show that the AEI signal is directly proportional to both current and acoustic pressure in the conditions adopted for this series of experiments.
In conclusion, these experiments have shown that it is possible to measure the AEI signal which results from ultrasound-induced changes in the conductivity of a conducting solution. It has been shown that it is neces- sary to determine and subtract ultrasound vibration potential eects if the AEI signal due to conductivity changes is to be measured accurately. These results show that the AEI signal is directly proportional to both current and acoustic pressure in the conditions adopted for this series of experiments. Downloaded to IP: 129.12.234.97 On: Mon, 24 Nov 2014 17:42:25  [SOURCE: 2007_Russel_witte_Imaging current flow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect.pdf | PAGE: 2] [FIGURE / TABLE caption detected on this page] Imaging current ﬂow in lobster nerve cord using the acoustoelectric effect Russell Witte,a Ragnar Olafsson, and Sheng-Wen Huang Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan 48109 Matthew O’Donnell Department of Bioengineering, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington 98195 Received 19 February 2007; accepted 15 March 2007; published online 17 April 2007 Ultrasound traversing a biologic ﬂuid or tissue generates a local change in electrical conductivity known as the acoustoelectric effect. The authors exploit this interaction to image ionic current injected into the abdominal segment of the lobster nerve cord. A pair of recording electrodes detected the acoustoelectric signal induced by pulses of focused ultrasound 1.4 or 7.5 MHz. The signal was linear with injected current at 2 MPa 0.7 V/mA cm2 and pressure at 75 mA/cm2 23 V/MPa.
The authors exploit this interaction to image ionic current injected into the abdominal segment of the lobster nerve cord. A pair of recording electrodes detected the acoustoelectric signal induced by pulses of focused ultrasound 1.4 or 7.5 MHz. The signal was linear with injected current at 2 MPa 0.7 V/mA cm2 and pressure at 75 mA/cm2 23 V/MPa. Acoustoelectric imaging of biocurrents potentially enhances spatial resolution of traditional electrophysiology and merits further study as an imaging modality for neural applications. © 2007 American Institute of Physics.
The signal was linear with injected current at 2 MPa 0.7 V/mA cm2 and pressure at 75 mA/cm2 23 V/MPa. Acoustoelectric imaging of biocurrents potentially enhances spatial resolution of traditional electrophysiology and merits further study as an imaging modality for neural applications. © 2007 American Institute of Physics. DOI: 10.1063/1.2724901 Of the 2.5106 people in the United States who suffer from epilepsy, approximately 150 000 are candidates for sur- gical treatment.1 Interventional neurosurgery usually requires laborious, pinpoint mapping of cortical function character- ized by local ﬁeld and action potentials to discern eloquent from dormant cortex prior to resection.
Acoustoelectric imaging of biocurrents potentially enhances spatial resolution of traditional electrophysiology and merits further study as an imaging modality for neural applications. © 2007 American Institute of Physics. DOI: 10.1063/1.2724901 Of the 2.5106 people in the United States who suffer from epilepsy, approximately 150 000 are candidates for sur- gical treatment.1 Interventional neurosurgery usually requires laborious, pinpoint mapping of cortical function character- ized by local ﬁeld and action potentials to discern eloquent from dormant cortex prior to resection. Although electroen- cephalogy detects synchronous neural activity from multiple electrodes on the skull, spatial resolution is typically limited to 1 cm or greater.
© 2007 American Institute of Physics. DOI: 10.1063/1.2724901 Of the 2.5106 people in the United States who suffer from epilepsy, approximately 150 000 are candidates for sur- gical treatment.1 Interventional neurosurgery usually requires laborious, pinpoint mapping of cortical function character- ized by local ﬁeld and action potentials to discern eloquent from dormant cortex prior to resection. Although electroen- cephalogy detects synchronous neural activity from multiple electrodes on the skull, spatial resolution is typically limited to 1 cm or greater. Subdural and penetrating electrodes, on the other hand, substantially improve precision at the cost of invasiveness.
DOI: 10.1063/1.2724901 Of the 2.5106 people in the United States who suffer from epilepsy, approximately 150 000 are candidates for sur- gical treatment.1 Interventional neurosurgery usually requires laborious, pinpoint mapping of cortical function character- ized by local ﬁeld and action potentials to discern eloquent from dormant cortex prior to resection. Although electroen- cephalogy detects synchronous neural activity from multiple electrodes on the skull, spatial resolution is typically limited to 1 cm or greater. Subdural and penetrating electrodes, on the other hand, substantially improve precision at the cost of invasiveness. In seeking an alternative solution that poten- tially preserves spatial resolution and minimizes invasive- ness of conventional electrical mapping, we exploit an acoustoelectric interaction between local pressure and den- sity to remotely detect current ﬂow in biologic tissue.
Although electroen- cephalogy detects synchronous neural activity from multiple electrodes on the skull, spatial resolution is typically limited to 1 cm or greater. Subdural and penetrating electrodes, on the other hand, substantially improve precision at the cost of invasiveness. In seeking an alternative solution that poten- tially preserves spatial resolution and minimizes invasive- ness of conventional electrical mapping, we exploit an acoustoelectric interaction between local pressure and den- sity to remotely detect current ﬂow in biologic tissue. This imaging approach potentially improves contrast and resolu- tion by constraining the detected signal to where the ultra- sound and current wave ﬁelds intersect. The acoustoelectric effect was initially reported by Fox et al.
In seeking an alternative solution that poten- tially preserves spatial resolution and minimizes invasive- ness of conventional electrical mapping, we exploit an acoustoelectric interaction between local pressure and den- sity to remotely detect current ﬂow in biologic tissue. This imaging approach potentially improves contrast and resolu- tion by constraining the detected signal to where the ultra- sound and current wave ﬁelds intersect. The acoustoelectric effect was initially reported by Fox et al. in 1946 to characterize colloids in solution.2 An acous- tic pressure wave P traveling in a biologic medium induces a local change in conductivity  given by d/ = k1dP, 1 with k1 an interaction constant on the order of 0.01% –0.1% /MPa in physiologic saline.3,4 When P inter- sects a current ﬁeld i in a uniform conducting medium, the change in conductivity leads to a voltage modulation V be- tween two recording electrodes of resistance R0 adjusted by an interaction constant k2, Vt = k2iR0Pt.
This imaging approach potentially improves contrast and resolu- tion by constraining the detected signal to where the ultra- sound and current wave ﬁelds intersect. The acoustoelectric effect was initially reported by Fox et al. in 1946 to characterize colloids in solution.2 An acous- tic pressure wave P traveling in a biologic medium induces a local change in conductivity  given by d/ = k1dP, 1 with k1 an interaction constant on the order of 0.01% –0.1% /MPa in physiologic saline.3,4 When P inter- sects a current ﬁeld i in a uniform conducting medium, the change in conductivity leads to a voltage modulation V be- tween two recording electrodes of resistance R0 adjusted by an interaction constant k2, Vt = k2iR0Pt. 2 Consequently, the phase and magnitude of the detected acoustoelectric voltage rely on the ultrasound pressure and applied current as well as the lead ﬁelds of the recording electrodes.
The acoustoelectric effect was initially reported by Fox et al. in 1946 to characterize colloids in solution.2 An acous- tic pressure wave P traveling in a biologic medium induces a local change in conductivity  given by d/ = k1dP, 1 with k1 an interaction constant on the order of 0.01% –0.1% /MPa in physiologic saline.3,4 When P inter- sects a current ﬁeld i in a uniform conducting medium, the change in conductivity leads to a voltage modulation V be- tween two recording electrodes of resistance R0 adjusted by an interaction constant k2, Vt = k2iR0Pt. 2 Consequently, the phase and magnitude of the detected acoustoelectric voltage rely on the ultrasound pressure and applied current as well as the lead ﬁelds of the recording electrodes. A more thorough derivation of the signal in an electrolyte solution has been described elsewhere.3 Recent studies using the acoustoelectric effect have been directed at medicine and biology.3,5,6 Acoustoelectric tomography, for example, has been proposed for high resolution electrical impedance imaging of breast tissue.5 We describe a system that measures Vt in neural tissue that depends on the ap- plied current distribution and location of the ultrasound source.
in 1946 to characterize colloids in solution.2 An acous- tic pressure wave P traveling in a biologic medium induces a local change in conductivity  given by d/ = k1dP, 1 with k1 an interaction constant on the order of 0.01% –0.1% /MPa in physiologic saline.3,4 When P inter- sects a current ﬁeld i in a uniform conducting medium, the change in conductivity leads to a voltage modulation V be- tween two recording electrodes of resistance R0 adjusted by an interaction constant k2, Vt = k2iR0Pt. 2 Consequently, the phase and magnitude of the detected acoustoelectric voltage rely on the ultrasound pressure and applied current as well as the lead ﬁelds of the recording electrodes. A more thorough derivation of the signal in an electrolyte solution has been described elsewhere.3 Recent studies using the acoustoelectric effect have been directed at medicine and biology.3,5,6 Acoustoelectric tomography, for example, has been proposed for high resolution electrical impedance imaging of breast tissue.5 We describe a system that measures Vt in neural tissue that depends on the ap- plied current distribution and location of the ultrasound source. We have previously demonstrated that the sensitivity of acoustoelectric detection in a simulated environment approaches current densities found in the human heart.7 A serial three-compartment nerve chamber, traditionally used to stimulate and record from frog and invertebrate tis- sue, was used to generate and detect the acoustoelectric sig- nal Fig.

A more thorough derivation of the signal in an electrolyte solution has been described elsewhere.3 Recent studies using the acoustoelectric effect have been directed at medicine and biology.3,5,6 Acoustoelectric tomography, for example, has been proposed for high resolution electrical impedance imaging of breast tissue.5 We describe a system that measures Vt in neural tissue that depends on the ap- plied current distribution and location of the ultrasound source. We have previously demonstrated that the sensitivity of acoustoelectric detection in a simulated environment approaches current densities found in the human heart.7 A serial three-compartment nerve chamber, traditionally used to stimulate and record from frog and invertebrate tis- sue, was used to generate and detect the acoustoelectric sig- nal Fig. 1a.
A more thorough derivation of the signal in an electrolyte solution has been described elsewhere.3 Recent studies using the acoustoelectric effect have been directed at medicine and biology.3,5,6 Acoustoelectric tomography, for example, has been proposed for high resolution electrical impedance imaging of breast tissue.5 We describe a system that measures Vt in neural tissue that depends on the ap- plied current distribution and location of the ultrasound source. We have previously demonstrated that the sensitivity of acoustoelectric detection in a simulated environment approaches current densities found in the human heart.7 A serial three-compartment nerve chamber, traditionally used to stimulate and record from frog and invertebrate tis- sue, was used to generate and detect the acoustoelectric sig- nal Fig. 1a. The abdominal segment of a fresh lobster nerve cord was excised and placed in the nerve chamber via a notch in the center divides.
We have previously demonstrated that the sensitivity of acoustoelectric detection in a simulated environment approaches current densities found in the human heart.7 A serial three-compartment nerve chamber, traditionally used to stimulate and record from frog and invertebrate tis- sue, was used to generate and detect the acoustoelectric sig- nal Fig. 1a. The abdominal segment of a fresh lobster nerve cord was excised and placed in the nerve chamber via a notch in the center divides. The intact nerve extended from the ﬁrst to the third chamber, each containing lobster Ring- er’s solution 462 mM NaCl, 16 mM KCl, 26 mM CaCl2, 8 mM MgCl2, 10 mM tris, 10 mM maleic acid, and 11 mM glucose. The middle compartment was sealed with petro- leum jelly and ﬁlled with mineral oil to electrically isolate the three chambers.
The abdominal segment of a fresh lobster nerve cord was excised and placed in the nerve chamber via a notch in the center divides. The intact nerve extended from the ﬁrst to the third chamber, each containing lobster Ring- er’s solution 462 mM NaCl, 16 mM KCl, 26 mM CaCl2, 8 mM MgCl2, 10 mM tris, 10 mM maleic acid, and 11 mM glucose. The middle compartment was sealed with petro- leum jelly and ﬁlled with mineral oil to electrically isolate the three chambers. A voltage source Agilent, Inc. 33120A and power am- pliﬁer Thorlabs, Inc. model MDT694 were used to generate a 300 Hz sinusoidal current wave form that was injected through a pair of AgCl electrodes placed in chambers 1 and 3.
A voltage source Agilent, Inc. 33120A and power am- pliﬁer Thorlabs, Inc. model MDT694 were used to generate a 300 Hz sinusoidal current wave form that was injected through a pair of AgCl electrodes placed in chambers 1 and 3. Current levels were monitored with a multimeter and in- dependently veriﬁed by sensing the voltage across a 1  resistor placed in series with the electrodes. Current density was estimated from the nerve cross section determined from pulse-echo images. The acoustoelectric signal was captured using a pair of high impedance tungsten recording electrodes placed in the middle chamber. They were separated by 10 mm and touched the exterior of the nerve without pen- etrating its protective outer sheath. The signals were fed to a differential ampliﬁer Lecroy, Inc.
The acoustoelectric signal was captured using a pair of high impedance tungsten recording electrodes placed in the middle chamber. They were separated by 10 mm and touched the exterior of the nerve without pen- etrating its protective outer sheath. The signals were fed to a differential ampliﬁer Lecroy, Inc. model 1855A, high pass ﬁlter, and second ampliﬁer Panametrics, Inc. model 5072PR, producing a total gain up to 79 dB. A fast 12 bit data acquisition board Signatec, Inc. model PDA12 digi- tized the signal. The acoustoelectric signal was generated using a dual- element confocal ultrasound transducer Etalon, Inc. fo- cused at 50 mm with a 7.5 MHz inner element for imaging and a calibrated 1.4 MHz outer element. The f numbers for the elements were 4 and 2, respectively. The transducer was submerged in de-ionized water.
The acoustoelectric signal was generated using a dual- element confocal ultrasound transducer Etalon, Inc. fo- cused at 50 mm with a 7.5 MHz inner element for imaging and a calibrated 1.4 MHz outer element. The f numbers for the elements were 4 and 2, respectively. The transducer was submerged in de-ionized water.
